text: 55.5g of fat. saturated fat, omega3, and polyunsaturated fat are doing most of the work. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.
18.8g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for a nut, this is mostly about fat quality and support.
21.2g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein is support here, not the whole argument.
vitamin e at 40% daily value stands out most. that gives this food real vitamin support.
magnesium at 61% daily value, zinc at 21% daily value, iron at 20% daily value stand out most. that gives this food real mineral support.

on the plus side: still a recognisable whole-food option because that keeps them cleaner than most processed snacks. holds up as a better snack default than most packaged options because that real-world swap value counts. fat and texture help satiety because they can hold up better than many snack foods.

the drawbacks next: spread form makes overdoing it easier because still good, but less self-limiting than whole nuts. portion control can get slippery fast because dense foods punish casual handful math. calorie density is very high because that makes portion control a real issue.
B tier. Best in small amounts where the fats and minerals matter more than sheer calories.
